entry.title: A philosophical take on "expiry"
entry.summary:
The post introduces a novel approach to managing blockchain transactions through what is termed as an "input-triggered expiry," which differs significantly from traditional expiry methods. This concept utilizes `nSequence` and `nLockTime` to condition a transaction based on the confirmations of its inputs within a specified timeframe. The innovation here lies in its ability to reinforce existing states if they meet the required conditions, keep transactions valid under potential future states, or render them unconfirmable if they cannot possibly meet these conditions in any forward progression of the blockchain.
Unlike traditional expiry mechanisms that necessitate a state of non-existence after a certain time, input-triggered expiry allows transactions to claim a state visible and verifiable at a given moment—specifically, the spent status of a coin at an outpoint with confirmed transactions by a predetermined time. This aligns with the philosophical stance that all operational states should be observable and quantifiable, thereby reducing ambiguity and enhancing network reliability.
The core argument extends beyond technical implementation to address philosophical concerns regarding the visibility and verifiability of transaction states. Traditional expiry proposals often introduce uncertainties since they involve states ("not-being") that are not directly observable and verifiable at the time of the transaction. By contrast, the proposed input-triggered mechanism ensures that all claimed states are within the realm of what can be currently observed or known, thus adhering to a more tangible and reliable framework.
This method also presents a solution to the "free relay" problem, where nodes may relay transactions that eventually become invalid, causing unnecessary network load without any cost to the attacker. By ensuring that only transactions with observable states are processed, the network inherently discourages such spam activities, promoting a healthier and more secure transaction environment.
In conclusion, the philosophy of visible and verifiable states not only enhances the technical robustness of the blockchain but also aligns with broader principles of historical accuracy and consensus building within the community. By focusing on what can be concretely seen and confirmed, the blockchain remains a reliable ledger of transactions, reflecting a true and agreed-upon history that supports consensus and minimizes disputes over transaction validity.
